item.title: Panama Canal Sees Revenue Beating Forecast After Hormuz Closure
item.description: The Panama Canal expects revenue to exceed its $5.2 billion forecast for fiscal 2026 after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz drove more ships through the waterway connecting the Caribbean Sea and the Pacific Ocean.

item.title: Traders Pare ECB Rate Bets to Below Quarter-Point Hike in 2026
item.description: Traders are reappraising the scope for the European Central Bank to lift interest rates as sliding oil prices reduce risks of sticky inflation.

item.title: US Stocks Post First Outflow Since March as Tech Trade Falters
item.description: Investors pulled money from US equities for the first time in three months, with record withdrawals from tech funds signaling that the artificial-intelligence trade is cooling.

item.title: US Stock Futures Resume Declines as AI Concerns Stall Tech Rally
item.description: US technology stocks were poised to drop, tracking sharp declines in Asian peers as investors remained skeptical about high artificial-intelligence valuations.

item.title: Norway Retail Sales Fall Most in Two Years, Dented by Holidays
item.description: Norwegian shoppers reduced their purchases by the most in almost two years in May, suggesting growing caution by households even as the data was affected by the timing of holidays.

item.title: Omnicom Chief Defends Interpublic Deal as AI Rattles Ad Industry
item.description: John Wren, the chief executive officer of ad giant Omnicom Group Inc., said he’s starting to see results after closing the deal for rival Interpublic Group about seven months ago — racking up customer wins and cost savings. He’s still got to convince the market.

item.title: OHB Shares Dip After German Satellite Firm’s €789 Million Re-IPO
item.description: Executives from German satellite firm OHB SE and KKR & Co. rang the opening bell at the Frankfurt Stock Exchange on Friday after they raised €789 million in a share sale to increase investors’ access to the space company.

item.title: Bain, LY Near a Binding Offer for Kakaku in Bidding War With EQT
item.description: Bain Capital and LY Corp. are poised to make a binding bid for Kakaku.com Inc., challenging a tender offer from EQT AB that values the price-comparison site operator at around ¥595 billion ($3.7 billion).

item.title: Barratt Redrow Leads UK Homebuilder Rally Amid Call for Buybacks
item.description: One name stands out in this week’s strong rebound in UK homebuilders’ stocks — Barratt Redrow Plc.

item.title: Indonesia Set to Boost Cash Placed in State Banks to $22 Billion
item.description: The Indonesian government will put an estimated 400 trillion rupiah ($22 billion) of its standby funds in state-owned lenders to keep on fueling economic growth, according to its finance chief.

item.title: Australian Banks Given Initial Nod to Distribute Cash In Reform
item.description: Australian banks got initial regulatory approval to be able to directly distribute notes and coins around the economy from next year, expanding the parties that are able to do so in the country.

item.title: Rory Johnston on Why His $200 Oil Prediction Didn't Turn Out Right
item.description: What the Iran War has taught us about the oil market.

item.title: ECB Says Consumers’ Short-Term Price Expectations Fall Sharply
item.description: Inflation expectations among euro-area consumers for the next year fell significantly in May, even before a deal was reached to end the hostilities in the Middle East.

item.title: Odd Lots: Rory Johnston on Why Oil Didn’t Hit $200 (Podcast)
item.description: The Strait of Hormuz has (mostly) re-opened! Crude prices are still up since the start of the war with Iran, but popular predictions earlier this year of $200-a-barrel Brent didn’t pan out. Why is that? We last talked to Rory Johnston, the founder of the Commodity Context newsletter, at the start of the conflict. And in that conversation he said that the Strait’s closure would lead to $200 oil if it persisted for any length of time. Today, he returns to tell us what he’s learned about the oil ma

item.title: World Bank, AfDB to Boost African Electrification Plan This Year
item.description: The World Bank and African Development Bank plan to accelerate their program to bring electricity to hundreds of millions of Africans this year by approving new projects, investing in Eritrea and promoting the development of regional power pools.

item.title: European Stocks Slip on Renewed Tech Woes; Zalando Slumps
item.description: European shares dropped from a record as jitters resurfaced over overvalued technology stocks.

item.title: T. Rowe’s Chung Sees Pullback in ‘Expensive’ Yuan After Rally
item.description: T. Rowe Price is betting on a pullback in the yuan, arguing that it looks “expensive” against a basket of currencies from China’s major trading partners.

item.title: Falling Inflation Expectations Take Pressure Off Bank of England
item.description: Inflation expectations among UK households followed oil prices lower in June, the latest sign the Bank of England may be able to avoid hiking interest rates.

item.title: Navis Explores Sale of Stake in Nuts and Snacks Firm Dan-D Foods
item.description: Navis Capital Partners is planning to sell its majority stake in nuts and snacks company Dan-D Foods Ltd.

item.title: China Gives Coal Room to Grow in New Five-Year Energy Plan
item.description: China is leaving room for coal consumption to grow in coming years, as the stability of the world’s largest energy market continues to outweigh climate concerns.

item.title: Key Kazakh Field Cuts Oil Output After Drones Hit Russian Plant
item.description: Kazakhstan’s giant Karachaganak oil and gas field cut crude production by more than a quarter after a drone attack forced the shutdown of a processing plant in Russia that handles its gas.

item.title: Emerging Market Stocks Slide Most in Three Weeks on Tech Selloff
item.description: Emerging-market stocks slumped the most in almost three weeks, dragged lower by a global tech selloff that hammered South Korean equities and triggered a 20-minute trading halt.

item.title: European Natural Gas Edges Higher as Traders Monitor Hormuz Risk
item.description: European natural gas prices inched higher after an attack on a cargo ship in the Strait of Hormuz renewed fears about safe passage through the critical waterway.

item.title: Palm Oil Climbs on Higher Exports and Indian Demand Expectations
item.description: Palm oil advanced on rising Malaysian exports and expectations of better demand from the biggest buyer India as festival season nears.

item.title: Lescure Sticks With 5% French Deficit After Watchdog Rebuke
item.description: Finance Minister Roland Lescure reaffirmed his commitment to lower France’s deficit after the country’s fiscal watchdog rebuked the government’s pending plans.

item.title: Zalando Plunges as Regulator Probes Retailer’s Accounts
item.description: Zalando SE plunged after Germany’s financial regulator opened a probe into the online fashion retailer’s 2025 report over suspected violations of accounting rules.

item.title: Traffic Flows Through Hormuz Despite Shock Container Ship Attack
item.description: An attack on a container vessel sailing through the Strait of Hormuz has prompted some shipowners to review exit plans, but traffic continued to flow in both directions through the vital thoroughfare on Friday.

item.title: The AI Trade Is No Longer About Owning One Thing: Taking Stock
item.description: The case for the artificial-intelligence trade remains intact, but the risk of getting it wrong has risen considerably. This week showed just how unforgiving the theme has become.

item.title: FTSE 100 Futures Fall as Tech Weakness Hits Sentiment
PBOC May Set New Overnight Operation Rate at 1.35%, Survey Shows
item.description: China’s central bank is expected to price its inaugural overnight reverse repo operation slightly below the seven-day rate when it debuts the tool on Monday, a Bloomberg survey shows.
